worth_it_explanation: Ad quality scanning is the layer that keeps malware, auto-redirects, and policy-violating creatives off a publisher's pages. A creative that hijacks the page or forces a redirect costs the publisher reader trust and, if it persists, demand-partner standing. The check is a review of the scanning controls a publisher already has access to through TripleLift and its Prebid wrapper, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete control the publisher can confirm is on and acted on.
intro: Ad quality scanning is the set of controls that inspect a creative before or as it renders, looking for malware, forced redirects, heavy or auto-playing content, and policy violations. On TripleLift, which carries native, branded-content, and connected TV (CTV) formats, a creative that misbehaves reaches the reader inside the publisher's own page. This subcategory is about whether scanning is enabled across the inventory TripleLift fills and whether the publisher acts on what the scanning flags. These checks confirm that scanning is a live control rather than a setting assumed to be on, and that flagged creatives are blocked or removed rather than left to keep serving.

audit_checks.title: Creative scanning for malware, redirects, and policy violations is not confirmed as enabled
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A creative that carries malware or forces an auto-redirect reaches the reader inside the publisher's page, which damages reader trust directly and can draw browser or platform penalties against the site. When scanning is not confirmed as enabled across the inventory TripleLift fills, malicious or policy-violating creatives can serve unchecked, so the gap exposes the publisher to harm that a scanning layer is built to catch.

educate.base:
Ad quality scanning inspects a creative for the behaviors a publisher does not want on the page. A malicious creative may carry malware, force an auto-redirect that pulls the reader off the site, or run heavy code that slows the page. A policy-violating creative may break the publisher's own rules on category, format, or sensitive content. TripleLift carries native, branded-content, and connected TV (CTV) formats, and a creative that misbehaves in any of them renders inside the publisher's own page where the reader experiences it as the publisher's problem.

Scanning happens at more than one layer, so the audit question is whether any of them is actually on for the inventory TripleLift fills. TripleLift runs its own creative review on the demand it sources, and the detail of how a publisher configures that review lives in the partner portal. When TripleLift fills through a Prebid header-bidding wrapper, the wrapper adds its own controls. Prebid Server can run a pre-auction scanning module that inspects each bid response for security and quality issues and tags it as clean, flagged, or skipped. Prebid.js can validate a bid response against the declared parameters and reject bids whose category, advertiser domain, or creative attributes do not match. A secure ad container such as SafeFrame isolates the creative from the publisher page so a malicious creative cannot reach page data or force a redirect from inside the frame.

The risk a publisher carries when scanning is not confirmed is that a malicious or non-compliant creative serves with nothing inspecting it. The harm is not gradual. A single forced-redirect creative can send readers off the site in a way they notice and remember, and a malware creative can draw a browser warning against the domain. A publisher who cannot say which scanning layer is on for TripleLift inventory has no evidence that the layer is doing its job.

A few signs point to scanning that is not confirmed as enabled:
  • No record exists of whether TripleLift's creative review settings have been reviewed in the partner portal for this inventory
  • The Prebid wrapper that carries TripleLift demand has no ad quality scanning module or bid response filter configured
  • Creatives render outside a secure container such as SafeFrame, so a misbehaving creative can reach the publisher page directly
  • The publisher cannot point to any layer that inspects TripleLift creatives before they serve
You may want to confirm that at least one scanning layer is enabled and covers the inventory TripleLift fills. One approach is to review the creative review settings in the TripleLift partner portal, then check whether the Prebid wrapper carrying TripleLift demand runs a scanning module, a bid response filter, or a secure container.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, readers arrive expecting editorial content, so a forced-redirect or malware creative is experienced as a breach of the site itself rather than a third-party problem. You may want to confirm scanning covers the high-traffic article and section-front placements first since a malicious creative there reaches the most readers before anyone notices.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, a creative that redirects a shopper away from a product or category page interrupts a path toward a purchase, which costs the publisher the session as well as the reader trust. You may want to confirm scanning covers the commercial pages, where a misbehaving creative does the most damage to both the reader and the publisher's own conversion.

learn_more.source_title: Confiant Ad Quality Module for Prebid Server

educate.learn_more_note: TripleLift's detailed creative review and ad quality configuration documentation is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review whether any scanning layer is enabled for the inventory TripleLift fills, covering the TripleLift creative review settings, the Prebid wrapper controls, and whether creatives render in a secure container.

triplelift_portal.label: TripleLift partner portal
steps: Sign in to the TripleLift partner portal and locate the creative review or ad quality settings for the relevant publisher account.
Record whether creative review is enabled, which formats it covers, and what categories or behaviors it screens for.
Note any setting that is off or left at a default the publisher cannot explain, and capture which inventory the settings apply to.

prebid_config.label: Prebid wrapper configuration
steps: Open the Prebid configuration that carries TripleLift demand and check whether a Prebid Server ad quality scanning module is configured, for example the Confiant Ad Quality module.
Check whether a Prebid.js bid response filter is configured to validate category, advertiser domain, and creative attributes against the declared parameters.
Record whether creatives render inside a secure container such as SafeFrame, and which bidders, if any, are set to render outside it.
Note any inventory carrying TripleLift demand where no scanning module, bid response filter, or secure container applies.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records which scanning layers are enabled for the inventory TripleLift fills, covering the TripleLift creative review settings, any Prebid scanning module or bid response filter, and whether creatives render in a secure container. Inventory where at least one layer inspects TripleLift creatives before they serve passes. Inventory where no layer is confirmed, so creatives can serve uninspected, is a fail recorded with the inventory and the layers that are missing.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record which scanning layers are enabled for TripleLift inventory above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} cannot confirm a creative scanning layer is enabled across the inventory TripleLift fills, so malicious or policy-violating creatives can serve with nothing inspecting them. A creative that carries malware or forces an auto-redirect reaches the reader inside the page, which damages reader trust and can draw a browser warning against the domain, and TripleLift carries native, branded-content, and connected TV (CTV) formats that all render in the publisher's own page. {{voice_recommend}} confirming the creative review settings in the TripleLift partner portal, then checking whether the Prebid wrapper carrying TripleLift demand runs an ad quality scanning module, a bid response filter, or a secure container such as SafeFrame. After the review, keep at least one scanning layer enabled on every slice of inventory TripleLift fills.

audit_checks.title: Flagged creatives are left active rather than blocked or removed
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Scanning that flags a malicious or non-compliant creative does no good if the flag goes unread and the creative keeps serving. A flagged creative left active continues to reach readers for as long as it runs, so the gap turns a working detection layer into a record of harm that nobody stopped, which is a follow-through failure rather than a detection failure.

educate.base:
Detection and response are two separate jobs. A scanning layer flags a creative as malicious, redirecting, or policy-violating, but flagging is only the first half. The creative stops doing harm when the publisher blocks it, removes it, or routes the flag to TripleLift so the demand is cut off. A flag that lands in a log nobody reads leaves the creative serving, and the reader keeps experiencing the behavior the scanning was meant to catch.

The layers that flag creatives also offer a path to act on them. A Prebid Server scanning module tags each bid response as clean, flagged, or skipped and sends those tags to analytics, where a publisher can see which creatives and which bidders are being caught. A Prebid.js bid response filter goes further and rejects a bid outright when its category, advertiser domain, or creative attributes fail validation, so the flagged creative never renders. Inside the TripleLift partner portal, a publisher can review flagged creatives and the demand sources behind them. Each of these turns a flag into an action only if someone follows the flag to its end.

The audit question is whether a flag leads anywhere. A publisher who scans but never reviews the output has a detection layer running with no response behind it, which is close to having no scanning at all from the reader's point of view. The creative that triggered the flag served, serves, and will keep serving until someone acts. Confirming that flags are read on a regular cadence, and that flagged creatives are blocked or escalated, is what closes the loop.

A few signs point to flags that are not acted on:
  • Scanning is enabled but no one reviews the flagged-creative output on any regular cadence
  • Flagged creatives from prior scans are still eligible to serve because no block or removal followed the flag
  • The bid response filter is set to log rather than reject, so failing bids still render
  • No record exists of flagged demand being escalated to TripleLift for the demand source to be cut off
You may want to confirm that flagged creatives lead to a block, a removal, or an escalation rather than sitting in a log. One approach is to set a regular cadence for reviewing the scanning output, and to confirm the bid response filter rejects failing bids rather than only recording them.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a flagged creative that keeps serving across high-traffic article and section-front pages reaches a large share of readers before anyone acts. You may want to set a short review cadence for the scanning output so a flag on a heavily-served placement is acted on quickly rather than discovered after the fact.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, a flagged creative on a product or category page can redirect shoppers who were close to a purchase, so a flag left unacted compounds into lost sessions. You may want to prioritize the scanning output for the commercial pages, where acting on a flag protects both the reader and the publisher's own conversion path.

learn_more.source_title: Confiant Ad Quality Module for Prebid Server

educate.learn_more_note: TripleLift's flagged-creative review and demand-blocking workflow is documented in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review whether flagged creatives lead to a block, a removal, or an escalation, covering the scanning output cadence, the bid response filter mode, and the TripleLift flagged-creative workflow.

prebid_analytics.label: Prebid scanning output and analytics
steps: Open the analytics that receive the scanning module tags and check whether flagged-creative output is reviewed on any regular cadence.
Record how often the output is read, who reads it, and what action follows a flag.
Check whether the Prebid.js bid response filter is set to reject failing bids or only to log them, and note any failing bid that still renders.

triplelift_portal.label: TripleLift partner portal
steps: Sign in to the TripleLift partner portal and locate the flagged-creative or creative review output for the relevant publisher account.
Record whether flagged creatives have been blocked, removed, or escalated, or whether they remain eligible to serve.
Note any flagged creative or demand source that is still active with no action recorded against it.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, for the scanning layers in place, whether flagged creatives lead to a block, a removal, or an escalation and on what cadence the output is reviewed. A setup where flags are read regularly and flagged creatives are blocked or escalated passes. A setup where scanning runs but flags sit unread, or where the bid response filter only logs rather than rejects, so flagged creatives keep serving, is a fail recorded with the flags and the inventory affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether flagged creatives are blocked, removed, or escalated, and on what cadence the output is reviewed, above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} runs creative scanning on TripleLift inventory but does not act on what it flags, so flagged creatives stay eligible to serve. Detection and response are separate jobs, and a flag that lands in a log nobody reads leaves the creative reaching readers for as long as it runs, which turns a working scanning layer into a record of harm that nobody stopped. {{voice_recommend}} setting a regular cadence to review the scanning output, confirming the Prebid bid response filter rejects failing bids rather than only logging them, and escalating flagged demand to TripleLift so the source is cut off. After the change, recheck that flagged creatives lead to a block, a removal, or an escalation rather than sitting unread.
